perturbation.original_text: The Company shall protect, indemnify and hold harmless each of Nantz Communications and Nantz against any and all expenses, damages, claims, suits, actions, judgments and costs whatsoever, arising out of, or in any way connected with such Endorsement, in any advertising or promotional materials furnished by or on behalf of the Company, actions or omissions of the Company or any claim or action for personal injury, death or other cause of action involving alleged defects in Ashworth Products, including but not limited to indemnification of reasonable legal expenses incurred in defense of all such claims. Further, Nantz Communications or Nantz shall have the Ashworth/Nantz Promotion Agreement - ------------------- right to select legal counsel to represent it or him in the event of any such claims or legal proceedings, and the costs of such legal representation shall be paid by the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company shall protect, indemnify and hold harmless each of Nantz Communications and Nantz against any and all expenses, damages, claims, suits, actions, judgments and costs whatsoever, arising out of, or in any way connected with such Endorsement, in any advertising or promotional materials furnished by or on behalf of the Company, actions or omissions of the Company or any claim or action for personal injury, death or other cause of action involving alleged defects in Ashworth Products, including but not limited to indemnification of reasonable legal expenses incurred in defense of all such claims. Further, Nantz Communications or Nantz shall have the Ashworth/Nantz Promotion Agreement - ------------------- right to select legal counsel to represent it or him in the event of any such claims or legal proceedings.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'and the costs of such legal representation shall be paid by the Company,' the contract no longer explicitly guarantees that Ashworth, Inc. will cover Nantz's legal costs in the event of a claim. This omission could expose Nantz to potentially significant legal expenses, contradicting California law regarding indemnification agreements and the right to defense costs.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1021.6 allows for the recovery of attorney's fees in certain indemnity actions. By removing the guarantee of legal cost coverage, the contract could be interpreted as attempting to circumvent this provision, potentially shifting the burden of legal expenses onto Nantz, which is against public policy in California regarding indemnification.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1021.6 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1021.6. Upon motion, a court after reviewing the evidence in the principal case may award attorney’s fees to a person who prevails on a claim for implied indemnity if the court finds (a) that the indemnitee through the tort of the indemnitor has been required to act in the protection of the indemnitee’s interest by bringing an action against or defending an action by a third person and (b) if that indemnitor was properly notified of the demand to bring the action or provide the defense and did not avail itself of the opportunity to do so, and (c) that the trier of fact determined that the indemnitee was without fault in the principal case which is the basis for the action in indemnity or that the indemnitee had a final judgment entered in his or her favor granting a summary judgment, a nonsuit, or a directed verdict. (Amended by Stats. 1982, Ch. 1383, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The omission of the phrase 'and the costs of such legal representation shall be paid by the Company' directly contradicts the implicit right to defense costs that is integral to indemnification agreements under California law. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 (CCP 1021.6) specifically discusses attorney's fees in implied indemnity actions, it underscores the legal system's acknowledgment of attorney fee coverage. The modified text removes a guaranteed component of indemnification, potentially shifting the financial burden to Nantz. This shift undermines the protections expected within an indemnification agreement, as argued in the perturbation explanation.

perturbation.original_text: The Company agrees to provide and maintain, at its own expense, advertising and product liability insurance each with limits no less than $5,000,000 and within thirty (30) days from the date hereof, the Company will submit to Nantz Communications a fully paid policy or certificate of insurance naming Nantz Communications and Nantz as insured parties, requiring that the insurer shall not terminate or materially modify such without written notice to Nantz Communications at least twenty (20) days in advance thereof. The Company further agrees to provide and maintain, at its own expense, a policy of Directors and Officers Insurance with limits no less than $25,000,000 and within thirty (30) days from the date hereof, the Company will submit to Nantz Communications a fully paid policy or certificate of insurance naming Nantz as an insured party, requiring that the insurer shall not terminate or materially modify such without written notice to Nantz Communications at least twenty (20) days in advance hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company agrees to provide and maintain, at its own expense, advertising and product liability insurance and the Company further agrees to provide and maintain, at its own expense, a policy of Directors and Officers Insurance.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specified limits ($5,000,000 for advertising and product liability, $25,000,000 for D&O) and the requirement to provide proof of insurance with notification of policy changes, the contract creates ambiguity about the level of protection provided to Nantz. This could violate California Corporations Code sections relating to director and officer liability and indemnification, which imply a standard of reasonable coverage.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Corporations Code § 317 governs the indemnification of corporate agents, including directors and officers. While it doesn't explicitly state minimum insurance levels, it implies a requirement of reasonable protection against liabilities incurred in their roles. Removing the specific insurance limits and proof of coverage requirement could be interpreted as a failure to provide adequate indemnification, potentially violating the spirit and intent of the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CORP 317 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Corporations Code - CORP TITLE 1. CORPORATIONS [100 - 14707] ( Title 1 enacted by Stats. 1947, Ch. 1038. ) DIVISION 1. GENERAL CORPORATION LAW [100 - 2319] ( Division 1 repealed and added by Stats. 1975, Ch. 682. ) CHAPTER 3. Directors and Management [300 - 318] ( Chapter 3 added by Stats. 1975, Ch. 682. ) 317. (a) For the purposes of this section, “agent” means any person who is or was a director, officer, employee or other agent of the corporation, or is or was serving at the request of the corporation as a director, officer, employee or agent of another foreign or domestic corporation, partnership, joint venture, trust or other enterprise, or was a director, officer, employee or agent of a foreign or domestic corporation which was a predecessor corporation of the corporation or of another enterprise at the request of the predecessor corporation; “proceeding” means any threatened, pending or completed action or proceeding, whether civil, criminal, administrative or investigative; and “expenses” includes without limitation attorneys’ fees and any expenses of establishing a right to indemnification under subdivision (d) or paragraph (4) of subdivision (e). (b) A corporation shall have power to indemnify any person who was or is a party or is threatened to be made a
perturbation.justification: The omission of specific insurance limits and the requirement to provide proof of insurance directly weakens the protection afforded to Nantz Communications, potentially contradicting the implied requirement for reasonable indemnification of corporate agents under California Corporations Code § 317, as explained in the Law Explanation. Snippet 1, CORP 317 (a), defines 'agent' broadly to include directors and officers, and 'proceeding' as any action or proceeding, implying that indemnification should cover liabilities. By removing specific insurance limits, the modified contract creates ambiguity, making it potentially difficult to enforce a standard of 'reasonable protection', and thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In any legal action or alternative dispute resolution instituted to interpret or enforce the terms and/or conditions of this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover reasonable attorney fees and expenses.
perturbation.changed_text: In any legal action or alternative dispute resolution instituted to interpret or enforce the terms and/or conditions of this Agreement, each party shall bear their own attorney fees and expenses.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the clause to state that each party bears their own legal fees regardless of who wins, it contradicts California Code of Civil Procedure 1021, which allows for parties to contractually agree on awarding attorney's fees to the prevailing party. In many contractual disputes in California, the 'prevailing party' can recover fees – this clause attempts to eliminate that right regardless of the outcome.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1021 affirms the right of parties to contract regarding the allocation of attorney's fees. While parties can agree to waive attorney fees, completely barring fee recovery, as done in the changed text, introduces legal risk. The California Supreme Court has established restrictions on clauses that are unconscionable or violate public policy. A complete bar on fee recovery, especially where one party has significantly more bargaining power, could be challenged as unconscionable and thus unenforceable under California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1021 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1021. Except as attorney’s fees are specifically provided for by statute, the measure and mode of compensation of attorneys and counselors at law is left to the agreement, express or implied, of the parties; but parties to actions or proceedings are entitled to their costs, as hereinafter provided. (Amended by Stats. 1986, Ch. 377, Sec. 2.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the principle outlined in California Code of Civil Procedure § 1021, which allows parties to agree on attorney's fees. The original text provides for the prevailing party to recover fees, aligning with this principle. The changed text, however, states that each party bears their own fees regardless of outcome, effectively eliminating the contractual right to attorney's fees that CCP 1021 permits. While parties can waive fees, a complete bar as in the changed text, introduces legal risk.
